exercises.title: Vocabulary size is a tradeoff
exercises.prompt: An LLM reads integers, not text. Explain the two-sided cost of vocabulary size: what goes wrong with a vocabulary that's too small, and what goes wrong with one that's too large. Why does this directly affect both sequence length and the embedding parameter budget?
exercises.hint: Small vocab → long token sequences (more compute, shorter effective context). Large vocab → huge embedding/output matrices (wasted params, rare tokens undertrained). Sweet spot ~32k-128k.
exercises.solution:
**Too small a vocabulary:** each piece of text gets split into **many tokens** (in the extreme, character- or byte-level). Consequences:
- **Longer sequences** → attention is O(N²), so more compute per example and **fewer documents fit** in a fixed context window (your effective context shrinks).
- The model must spend capacity **re-learning** how to assemble common words from fragments every time.

**Too large a vocabulary:** 
- The **embedding matrix and the output (softmax) projection** are both `vocab_size × d_model`. Doubling the vocab doubles those matrices — **wasted embedding parameters** that could have gone to depth/width.
- **Rare tokens are undertrained:** with millions of tokens, many appear too infrequently to learn good embeddings, so they're noise.
- The final softmax over a huge vocab is more expensive.

**Why it touches both sequence length and params:** vocabulary size sets the **compression ratio** (text → tokens). A bigger vocab packs more characters per token → **shorter sequences** (cheaper attention, longer effective context) but **bigger embedding/output matrices**. A smaller vocab is the reverse. So you're trading **sequence-length efficiency** against **parameter efficiency** — which is why production tokenizers cluster around **32k–128k** tokens: large enough to keep sequences short and words intact, small enough that embeddings stay affordable and well-trained.

exercises.title: BPE vs WordPiece vs Unigram
exercises.prompt: Compare the three dominant subword algorithms — BPE, WordPiece, and Unigram (SentencePiece) — on how each builds its vocabulary. Implement the core BPE merge loop, and explain what 'merge by frequency' (BPE) vs 'merge by likelihood' (WordPiece) vs 'prune from a large set' (Unigram) means.
exercises.hint: BPE: greedily merge the most frequent adjacent pair, repeat. WordPiece: merge the pair that most increases corpus likelihood. Unigram: start with a huge vocab, iteratively remove tokens that hurt likelihood least.

exercises.title: Tokenization artifacts and why they cause real bugs
exercises.prompt: Tokenization quirks cause concrete model failures. Explain why LLMs miscount the R's in 'strawberry', struggle with arithmetic and reversing strings, and tokenize code and non-English text inefficiently. Then explain byte-level fallback and how a multilingual/code-heavy tokenizer mitigates (but doesn't eliminate) these issues.
exercises.hint: Models see tokens, not characters: 'strawberry' may be 2-3 tokens, so per-character tasks are blind. Digits chunk inconsistently. Non-Latin scripts/code get many tokens (poor compression → cost + worse quality). Byte-level fallback guarantees coverage; balanced training data improves fairness.
exercises.solution:
**Why character-level tasks fail (the 'strawberry' problem):** the model never sees individual characters — it sees **token IDs**. 'strawberry' might tokenize as `straw` + `berry` (or similar), so when asked 'how many R's?', the model has no direct access to the letters inside those tokens; it would have to have *memorized* the spelling of each token. Counting characters, reversing strings, and similar per-character operations are **structurally hard** because the unit of perception is the token, not the letter. The model can sometimes fake it from training data, but it's unreliable.

**Why arithmetic is hard:** numbers tokenize **inconsistently** — '1234' might be one token, '12' + '34', or '123' + '4' depending on context and the merge rules. Digit groupings don't align with place value, so the model can't cleanly do column arithmetic; the same number appears as different token sequences in different contexts. (Modern tokenizers mitigate this by **splitting digits individually** — e.g. Llama, GPT-4 force single-digit tokens — which measurably improves arithmetic.)

**Why code and non-English are inefficient:** a tokenizer trained mostly on English web text learns merges for English words. **Code** (indentation, `camelCase`, operators, rare identifiers) and **non-Latin scripts** (CJK, Arabic, Indic) get few useful merges, so they fragment into **many tokens** — poor **compression ratio**. Consequences:
- **Cost & latency:** you pay per token, so the same sentence in Hindi or Thai can cost 2-4× more tokens than English — a real **fairness and pricing** problem.
- **Shorter effective context** for those languages/code (more tokens for the same content).
- **Worse quality:** more fragmentation = harder modeling, and rare-language tokens are undertrained.

**Byte-level fallback:** instead of an `<UNK>` token for anything out-of-vocabulary, the tokenizer can **fall back to raw UTF-8 bytes** (256 base byte tokens). This **guarantees any input is encodable** — every emoji, every script, every binary blob — with **no unknown tokens**, at the cost of more tokens for rare content. GPT-2/GPT-4 use byte-level BPE; Llama uses byte fallback in SentencePiece. It eliminates the 'unknown token' class of bugs entirely.

**How a multilingual/code-heavy tokenizer mitigates (not eliminates):** train the tokenizer on a **balanced, representative corpus** (proportional non-English text, lots of code) so it learns useful merges for those domains → better compression, fairer cost, better quality. Force **digit-splitting** for arithmetic. Use **byte-level fallback** for universal coverage. But it **can't fully eliminate** the issues: there's a fixed vocab budget, so improving one language's compression often costs another's; per-character tasks remain hard because the fundamental unit is still the token; and any fixed tokenizer makes tradeoffs frozen at training time. The lesson's point — 'the tokenizer decides whether those integers carry meaning or waste it' — is that many 'model' failures are really **tokenizer** failures, and choosing/designing the tokenizer is a first-class modeling decision, not a preprocessing afterthought.
